Two domed support units stand on the upper part’s chest. One appears to be a high grade shield generator. The other provides mid-fight repair. Two sensors on its head gleam menacingly in your direction.

original: The patrol bot stumbles back from your strike, sparks flying from its joints. It staggers back and forth, then topples, narrow heels snapping as it loses its balance. One armored hip bounces against the metal floor, and the bot’s head hits next, making a loud crack as it strikes the deck. A few static-distorted words spill from the bot’s speaker, and a puff of smoke billows out of the neck before it goes still.
context: <<POS:33112721>> textify(['\\n\\tThe patrol bot stumbles back from your strike,'...])

original: You crumple to the ground, unable to keep fighting. The patrol bot that brought you down approaches, and tilts its head down at you, the lenses above its barely-featured face flickering in the Mastodon’s lights. The bot takes only a moment to raise its cannon and point it directly at your face. A bright white light ignites in the cannon’s depths. The bot booms, “REMAINING CREW DETECTED. DESTROY ALL REMAINING CREW.” The white glow of the cannon is the last thing you see. GAME OVER
context: <<POS:33113722>> textify(['\\n\\tYou crumple to the ground, unable to keep figh'...])

original: As soon as you’re back on your ship, you head for the cockpit and put in a call to Steele Tech’s recovery department. There’s no point in risking damaging the Mastodon further by trying to tow it yourself, and if something happens to your ship on the way back to Dhaal, it’ll be in even more trouble. Your call connects almost immediately; one of the privileges of being heir to the company, you suppose. The person who pops up on your screen looks like a male human — he’s tall, with a very prominent nose and a thick beard that matches his light brown hair. Only his grayish skin suggests some mild modding in his past. His expression makes him look like the type who’s always ready with a friendly joke. “Hey there, this is Steele Tech recovery, how can I—” The man’s warm, mellow voice cuts off as he realizes who he’s talking to. He blinks once. “Well damn, it’s the boss,” he says, then elbows someone off-screen. “Hey, Yalc, look who just popped up.” Another man leans over from next to the first one, this one with pink hair done in a side-shaved cut and long, goat-like ears. He looks at you and kind of chuckles, then leans back to whatever he was doing. The man who answered your call continues. “Anyway, hi there, [pc.mr] Steele, good to see you. My name’s Tamup Los. How can the recovery crew help you out today?” You tell Tamup that you need a towing ship at your coordinates, with something big enough to haul a cargo vessel. He nods as you send him the details, and asks a few questions about the other ship’s condition and whether or not you need an armed escort to go along with it. “Got that, and I think I can get a team to your location in a few hours,” Tamup Los says after taking down your info. “Where you headed with that thing?” You tell him you’re returning the ship to Dhaal, as it belongs to some... friends of yours. “Dhaal, eh?” Tamup types in some more info, then rolls his wide shoulders. “I heard some crazy stuff about that place. Is it true the native species is all girls, but they’ve got their parts in their tails?” You explain the Dhaal natives to Tamup, and he takes it all in without changing his amused expression at all. “Wow. I’ve heard some stuff about the Rush, I tell you what, but that’s a new one on me.” He laughs a little, shaking his head. “All right, [pc.mr] Steele, I’ve got a crew heading in your direction. Just sit tight and they should be there soon as the warp gate can get them through. I’ll reach out once I’ve got information about where you can park the thing on Dhaal. Anything else I can do for you today?” You tell Tamup that’s all you need for now. “All righty,” Tamup says with a friendly smile. “You stay safe out there.”
context: <<POS:33116576>> textify(['\\n\\tAs soon as you’re back on your ship, you head '...])

Tamup Los calls you again once you’re through the warp gate, and gives y...

<<POS:33129556>> addButton(e++,"Next",(function(){clearAll(),author("Slab Bulkhead"),showBust("KIKI","JOSIE","KARRAH"),output(textify(Xme||(Xme=(0,Ce.Z)(["\n\t\tYou head into the Dhaal spaceport with the Mastodon and the towing crew behind you. Tamup Los calls you again once you’re through the warp gate, and gives y...

original: You head into the Dhaal spaceport with the Mastodon and the towing crew behind you. Tamup Los calls you again once you’re through the warp gate, and gives you a heads-up on where he paid for the Mastodon to dock. He also lets you know the company’s only got two days covered for docking fees — the rest is up to, as he puts it, “your friends”. Once you have that info, you give the Crash Landing crew a call. Kiki answers, and you tell her you’ve made it back with the ship. Kiki’s eyes light up behind her glasses, and for a moment, she just stands there, looking utterly shocked. The dance room’s lights pass over her face a few times before she blinks. “Holy shit, you did it,” she breathes. “I’ll tell the others. We’ll be there soon as we can, but give us a minute — we’ve got to suit up to go outside.” She giggles. “This is amazing, see you soon!” After docking your ship, you head over to the port where the Mastodon is docked. It looks a little different in Dhaal’s eternal twilight, less like a grounded crew’s hope and more like just another half-wrecked ship. But you get the feeling the bar girls will see it differently. It’s nearly an hour before the former pirates show up. When you catch sight of them, you see why Kiki said it would take time for them to prepare — they look like they’re ready for war. Karrah is dressed mostly the same, though she has one enormous blaster rifle in her hands and another slung on her back, and her head’s on a swivel as she looks around, eyes narrowed and tusks bared. Monika wears some mixed armor over her usual outfit and carries her own rifle, and looks just as wary as Karrah, if not more. Lights blink in her mechanical eye, making it look like she’s scanning for targets. Kiki has changed out of her usual suit and is also wearing dark clothes under armor, though she looks seriously uncomfortable. A smaller version of Karrah’s moving cannon sits on her shoulder, and she has a pistol on each hip, though she doesn’t walk like she knows how to use them. The three women walk in a protective triangle around Josie, who’s shed her apron for a simple lavender sundress that clings to her curves like it’s afraid she’ll get away. The cowgirl looks nervous, and glances around as she walks, clearly afraid she’ll be kidnapped for her milk. Then again, anyone who tries to go for her deserves what they get, what with the armed former space pirates surrounding her. Once the crew sees you, they hurry over, and Monika gives you a once-over as she approaches. “Good to see you, [pc.name],” she says. “Glad you made it back alive.” The smile she wears has a little of the friendly bartender in it, but looks a lot more genuine than that. She looks beyond you to the Mastodon. “That’s her?” There’s a quiet moment as the crew looks at the Mastodon. The damage to the ship is significant, and you can only imagine how it must look to them. But it’s theirs now, and that counts for a lot. “I thought it’d be bigger,” Josie says quietly. Karrah grunts. “You say that about everything.” “Well, yeah,” Josie says, then steps out of the protective triangle, her eyes wide as she looks over the ship. After a moment, she turns around and holds her hands behind her back, a huge smile on her face. She bends over a little, showing a ridiculous amount of cleavage. “What should we name it?” “I’ve got a ton of ideas, but we can take a vote later,” Kiki says, a grin spreading across her dark face. “I’m already in the spaceport files, [pc.name], and I’m getting everything set up so the ship won’t be connected to you or the old crew or anything like that.” Lines of blue text fly down both lenses of her glasses as she talks. “Give it a few weeks, and everyone will swear this thing’s always been here.” “Weeks,” Monika muses, then gives Kiki a rueful look. “Any thoughts on how long it’ll take to fix this thing up?” You glance around as Kiki runs the numbers. Josie stays close, though she leans back and forth to get a better look at the Mastodon, every motion threatening to make her spill out of her dress. A nearby cluster of zaika starts looking at the cowgirl a little too closely, and Karrah turns and snarls at them until they find somewhere else to be. One of them gives Karrah herself a long look before she scurries away. “Yeah, that’s not good,” Kiki says after a long moment. She looks at Monika and sighs, the mirth gone from her face. “Okay. I figured in our usual budget, docking fees, repair work, repair crews because there’s no way we can do this all on our own, a paint job so it doesn’t look like the old ship, licensing and registration fees, a little under the table stuff to make sure nobody connects this thing to anybody, and a slush fund in case we have to bribe anyone....” Kiki trails off, then shakes her head, blue hair trailing back and forth over her shoulder cannon. “It’s going to take a long damn time to get this thing up and running again.” She gives Monika a grin. “But when it’s done, it’ll be ours.” “Fucking right it will be,” Karrrah says, clenching her huge green fist. “Can’t wait to get off this rock. And that thing’s pretty badass.” She reaches over and gives you a punch on the shoulder. “Not bad, [pc.name]. Not bad at all.” Monika slides her rifle onto a holder on her back, then turns to you. “I don’t know what to say,” she says, and there’s a softness to her smile that you’ve never seen outside her bedroom. “We haven’t been here too long, but it was starting to feel like we’d never get the chance to leave. And now... now we’ve got it.” She takes a deep breath. “We get to fly again. I don’t even know how to thank you for that.” Before you can say anything, Josie steps forward, and says, “If you’re not going to hug [pc.himHer], then I will.” She moves in close and wraps her arms around you, pulling you into a tight and amazingly soft hug. It’s very warm, and while you’re kind of surprised she doesn’t rub her chest against you, you suppose an occasion like this can make even a Treated girl just be thankful. She gives your [pc.ass] a quick squeeze as she pulls away, though, so clearly that moment doesn’t last too long. Josie then looks at you and says, a big smile on her face, “I can’t wait to think up new ways to fool people with my boobs, once we’re flying again.” She pauses, looking oddly thoughtful. “Though I’ll miss getting milked all the time.” “You can do that anyway,” Karrah points out. “We’ll help, you know that.” “Well, yeah, but it’s not the same as having a bunch of girls be all hot for it,” Josie says, and looks to Monika. “Before we leave, can we get some zaika for the new crew?” “Maybe, this thing can hold a lot more than just us,” Kiki says before Monika can answer. More lines of text flicker down the lenses of her glasses, along with an entire schematic. A devious grin spreads across her face. “But I need to get to work on plotting this. I can get so much modding done on this thing....” “Long as it gets us out of here,” Karrah mutters. She gives you another fanged grin. It’s plenty clear she’s happy with what you’ve done. “One thing at a time,” Monika says. “Is the ship secure here, Kiki? Nobody can claim it but us?” Kiki nods. “Already on that,” she says. “Far as anybody knows, this dock is reserved by the Chan Darlings, a small shipping group out of Tavros Station.” She giggles again. “They didn’t exist until an hour ago.” If she can do that, then why do they need to work up the credits to repair the ship? Why not just fill the fake company’s accounts with credits and get it all done? Kiki presses her lips together and looks sheepish, an expression that doesn’t seem to fit her at all. “I learned the hard way not to fuck around with credit laundering when I was first learning all this stuff,” she says. “Little things like transferring our funds into the Chan accounts, nobody cares. But if we start throwing around credits on a ship that just showed up, someone’s going to notice.” She looks around the spaceport. “Especially a bunch of off-worlders like us.” “I’m fine with playing it safe,” Monika says. “We’ve done this much, I don’t want to ruin it now that we’ve got a ship.” Another small smile reaches her lips. It looks like she likes being able to say that they have a ship again. Monika clears her throat, then looks at you again. “Thank you,” she says, utter sincerity in her voice. “I don’t think I can say that enough, even if it doesn’t feel like enough.” “We’ve always got more hugs and more sex,” Josie says. “And more booze. But I think you still have to pay for that.” “Anyway,” Monika says, “we should go.” She looks around at the rest of the crew. “Got a long time at the bar ahead of us before this thing’s spaceworthy again.” “She’ll get there,” Kiki says. “And thanks, [pc.name]. I am going to have so much fun working on this thing.” The crew finally finishes their goodbyes and takes up their arms, forming the protective triangle around Josie again as they head back into the city.

[flags.MASTODON_VISITED
		|You decide to seek out the Mastodon again. After you reach the jump gate, it’s another long journey to the empty part of space where the ship drifts.
		|You enter the coordinates Monika gave you, and your computer gives you a heading. It looks like Redd’s ship was on a route in between jump gates, so it’ll take some time to reach it.
	]

<<POS:33133576>> addButton(e++,"Next",(function(){clearAll(),author("Slab Bulkhead"),output(textify(mme||(mme=(0,Ce.Z)(["\n\t\t[flags.MASTODON_VISITED\n\t\t\t|The Mastodon floats through space, and there’s no sign anyone else has been here. original: [flags.MASTODON_VISITED |The Mastodon floats through space, and there’s no sign anyone else has been here. Debris clouds hover around the darkened thrusters, and lights still flicker across both halves of the abandoned Pyrite Starhammer. A quick scan still indicates nothing living aboard. |After you pass through the jump gate, it takes a few hours before you reach the coordinates for Redd’s ship. The space there is empty, so you set your computer to scan for anything nearby. After a few minutes of searching, you get a hit, and fly off in that direction. When you find the ship, it looks like nobody’s home. The vessel is a Pyrite Industries Starhammer, like Kiki said, done in plain gray, with large black letters across the rear half reading “THE MASTODON”. It’s tall and bulky, divided into two parts — a large rear section for holding cargo, and a smaller front section for the crew, with a narrow part connecting the two. It’s also been through hell. The small weapon bays on both sides look like they’ve been blown out, and lights flicker on and off all over the ship, suggesting electrical problems on board. Debris surrounds both of the darkened thrusters. The way it’s tilting suggests it’s been drifting for a while. There are docking bays on both sides, and they appear undamaged. A quick scan indicates nothing living aboard. ]

original:
You fly closer and prepare docking maneuvers. With the angle the ship’s drifting at and no one onboard to match your movement, it takes a moment to get your vessel properly aligned. Once you’ve docked, you head to your ship’s airlock.
			
			Readings from your airlock indicate that there’s breathable air on the Mastodon, but that’s about all you get. It’s clear that something bad happened here, but there’s no telling what’s waiting for you on the other vessel.
			
			Time to find out.

original:
The Mastodon’s starboard airlock entry is simple and utilitarian, just a basic open area for receiving people. All around you are steel walls and scuffed floors, marking the ship as a fairly standard cargo hauler, and one that’s seen a lot of use. The ceiling is a good twenty feet high, providing plenty of room for cargo. Running lights shine along the floor, directions to the different areas of the ship are marked on the walls, and black marks from blaster fire scar the walls and floor.
	
	The airlock itself stands open, leading back to your ship. 
	
	A video screen next to the entrance is still functional. It’s playing a video on repeat, with a rahn woman speaking. You can watch it if you want.
context: <<POS:33133756>> textify(['\\n\\tThe Mastodon’s starboard airlock entry is simp'...])

<<POS:33139173>> addButton(e++,"Video",(function(){clearAll(),author("Slab Bulkhead"),output(textify(kme||(kme=(0,Ce.Z)(["\n\t\tThe video flickers as it starts, then focuses on a red rahn woman. She’s tall and has a strong, sturdy figure, with modest curves and a stern face. Her ponytail is done in long spikes, and ...

original: The video flickers as it starts, then focuses on a red rahn woman. She’s tall and has a strong, sturdy figure, with modest curves and a stern face. Her ponytail is done in long spikes, and her short bangs are thick and spiky. She’s clad in a coat that makes her look official but also looks like she bought it second-hand, and a pair of armored pants. This must be Redd, one of the former captains of Monika’s original crew. She peers at the camera for a moment, then steps back. Behind her is the cargo bay of the Mastodon, with multiple crew members of various species moving crates of different sizes into place. The crew calls out to each other as they work, and the rahn glances over her shoulder, then turns back to the camera. “Mastodon travel log, number twenty-seven, date—” The video flickers and goes out for a moment, and when it comes back, Redd is leaning in close and glaring at the camera. “Fucking thing, I swore I fixed that.” She leans back again. “Anyway. This video will serve as proof of collection and preparation for the goods described in the attached contract, as per usual. We received seven containers from Pyrexxx Industreez labeled G4-L3, two labeled G4-B1, and three crates labeled as G4-Z1, G4-Z1-A, and G4-Z1-B.” She pauses, and a hint of concern passes over her crimson face. “As always, I’m not thrilled about hauling military-level hardware, especially stuff that sounds like cheap Pyrite bootlegs, but I’ll take what I can get.” She pauses for a moment, as though gathering her thoughts, then continues. “No problems to report with acquisition, and I’m hoping for an easy delivery. The new crew we picked up on Tarkus, Rowan and Val, have finished their probationary period—” She rolls her eyes as she says that phrase, and you get the impression she doesn’t think much of what sounds like an imposed technicality. “—and they’re now considered full members of the crew. This will show up as a change on our invoices, so now you’ve got two records of it, and I’ll gladly provide you with a third if you try to fuck me over upon delivery.” The woman sounds professional, but it’s clear she’s dealing with regulations she doesn’t like. Monika said Redd was trying to run a legit business, and while it seems like she has a good hold of it, the temptation to forget all of it and just sell the goods to the highest bidder must never go away. “Our estimated delivery time is in four or five days standard,” Redd continues. “My navigator warned me about possible traffic at the jump gates, and there’s an asteroid field we might have to deal with before then, so there might be some delays.” She gives the camera a small smile. “But we’ve got a good crew and a sturdy ship, so I think we can handle just about anything.” An enormous clatter rings out behind Redd, and she winces, then turns and storms into the cargo hold. “Who dropped what?” she yells. You can’t hear the other part of the conversation, but it sounds like several people all trying not to take the blame at once. “You don’t need to care what’s in them,” Redd says, her voice stern and loud. “Secure them and make sure everything’s accounted for. That’s it. Don’t let this happen again.” There’s a chorus of “Yes, captain!” as Redd storms back to the camera. Her coat has come partway open, and as she approaches, you catch sight of her belt buckle: a solid piece of shining metal with “RR” stamped on it in squarish letters. “I assure you,” Redd says as she closes her coat, “nothing was damaged. Everybody gets curious about what we’re hauling, that’s only natural, but I’ve set them straight.” She seems to collect herself again. “We’ll be launching within the hour, and I’ll send this video to the usual recipients, as well as make sure it’s available for anyone who needs to inspect the ship. If there are any questions regarding the shipment, you can contact me at the address that sent this thing.” Redd gives the camera a smirk that hints at the pirate captain she used to be. “As always, to the credit-counting insurance agents who make it so I have to record these stupid damn video logs, I fucking hate every one of you, and you can all suck the dick I’ll grow specifically for the occasion.” The video ends.
context: <<POS:33134606>> textify(['\\n\\t\\tThe video flickers as it starts, then focuse'...])

original: The Mastodon’s cockpit is built for efficiency, not comfort, and it’s no bigger than it needs to be. Three giant view screens take up most of the plain metal walls on the front part, showing the endless starfield outside. Two seats side by side, one for the pilot and one for the navigator, take up most of the rest of the space. The seats have clearly seen some wear, and one has strips of sealing tape holding the back of it together.\n\t\n\tCommunication panels sit to the side of both chairs, and weapon controls are built into both consoles. It looks like the ship was made to have two people flying it, but it’s doable with one.
context: <<POS:33139299>> textify(['\\n\\tThe Mastodon’s cockpit is built for efficiency'...])

original:
This is the ship’s bridge. Computer screens show the Mastodon’s schematics and list off any issues. They’re currently warning of the non-functional thrusters, along with multiple damage reports caused by the battles that took place on the ship. Other monitors display security camera feeds for the cargo bay and engine rooms, allowing the crew to keep track of the rear half of the ship from the front. Most of those screens are black or showing only flickering, distorted images. Several large chairs sit before the screens.
	
	The remains of the munitions bot lie in a smoking heap in the center of the room. Melted rubber from its tank treads pools around the wreckage, and the shielding and repair domes lay broken across the humanoid chest.
	
	With the robot dead, you can access the ship’s schematics. It looks like the Mastodon has taken a fair amount of damage, but it’s not a total loss. You’ll need to call someone to tow it, as it’s no longer capable of flight, but there’s enough left that it can be repaired and become spaceworthy again.
	
	A single bright light blinks on one of the communication screens. You look closer, and see that a video is available — clearly an important one, if the bright red text reading “PLAY” is any indication.
context: <<POS:33140055>> textify(['\\n\\tThis is the ship’s bridge. Computer screens sh'...])

original: Three heavily armed bots patrol the bridge of the Mastodon. Each robot consists of a humanoid upper-half with guns for arms--one a rapid firing minigun and the other a charged beam cannon--as well as two dome-shaped defensive units attached to the chest, all resting atop huge, moving rubberized tank treads. Their eye-shaped sensors flash a light of warning before they aim their hostile weaponry in your direction. You have no choice but to ready yourself. It’s a fight!
context: <<POS:33141484>> textify(['\\n\\tThree heavily armed bots patrol the bridge of '...])

original: Your final attack breaks something vital in the heavy munitions bot, and a blaze of sparks and lightning surges through its humanoid body. A fire starts somewhere within its lower half, spreading all through its mechanics and to the tank treads, which begin to melt. The humanoid body shakes itself to pieces, and falls apart all at once, the head spinning around until its neck snaps from the strain.\n\t\t\n\t\tFinally, the robot shudders and crumples to the ground, the lights across its torso and the glow from its beam cannon going dark. It settles into a heap of metal, broken and useless. It’s done.
context: <<POS:33142218>> textify(['\\n\\t\\tYour final attack breaks something vital in '...])

original: You crumple to the ground, unable to keep fighting. The monstrous bot rolls toward you on its wide treads, and tilts its head down at you, the dual lenses of its sensors flickering in the Mastodon’s lights. The robot takes only a moment to deploy its beam cannon and point it directly at your face. A bright blue light ignites in the cannon’s depths. The bot booms, “REMAINING CREW DETECTED. DESTROY ALL REMAINING CREW.” The blue glow of the cannon is the last thing you see. GAME OVER
context: <<POS:33143666>> textify(['\\n\\t\\tYou crumple to the ground, unable to keep fi'...])

			|Redd, the rahn woman from the earlier video standing in front of a motley crew of fellow spacers.
			|a red rah...

<<POS:33149512>> addButton(e++,"Video",(function(){clearAll(),author("Slab Bulkhead"),output(textify(Eme||(Eme=(0,Ce.Z)(["
		The video blinks to life, and you see [flags.MASTODON_CAPTAIN_LOG
			|Redd, the rahn woman from the earlier video standing in front of a motley crew of fellow spacers.
			|a red rah...

original: The video blinks to life, and you see [flags.MASTODON_CAPTAIN_LOG |Redd, the rahn woman from the earlier video standing in front of a motley crew of fellow spacers. |a red rahn woman standing in front of a motley crew of fellow spacers. She’s tall and has a strong, sturdy figure, with modest curves and a stern face. Her ponytail is done in long spikes, and her short bangs are thick and spiky. She’s clad in a coat that makes her look official but also looks like she bought it second-hand, and a pair of armored pants. This must be Redd, one of the captains of Monika’s original crew. ] Redd is holding the camera in her hand, and behind her, the rest of the crew is smiling and grinning and looking relieved. Redd herself looks very pleased. They’re all gathered in front of the escape pod bays in the rear half of the ship. “This is Captain Redd, and this is the last log of the Mastodon,” Redd says, the pleased smile on her face a sharp contrast to the words. It’s not what one would usually expect from a captain giving up their ship. “I don’t like to brag — okay, that’s a lie — but we just skirted the Black Void. Again.” A cheer rises from the crew, including a horned blue woman of indeterminate species and accent shouting “Motherfuckers!” “Once this log is done, I’ll send it to the bridge, but Monika, or anyone else from the old crew, if you’re watching this, we got away.” Redd takes a moment to settle herself, and continues. “We picked up someone back on Tarkus, a jackass named Rowan who turned out to be working for the Black Void and was here to screw us over. He took over the robots we were shipping and turned them on us, then tried to run before they killed all of us. We caught him in the secret passages when we were getting everyone together to make our escape.” Redd pauses. “At least, he said he was from the Void. Seems like their standards are going down if that’s who they sent for us. Maybe there was just a hit out and he wanted to sound like he was hot shit.” She smirks. “Either way, we put the fucker out the airlock a few minutes ago.” There’s another cheer from the crew, but it’s more subdued, and some of them look like they’re not sure if they should be happy about this. It seems like they weren’t ready for this kind of thing, and it’s not clear who among them knew about Redd’s history as a pirate before she tried going legit. “So fuck the Void, they’ll get nothing from us, and they couldn’t stop us,” Redd says. “But we’re getting out of here. The escape pods can get us somewhere safe, and from there, we’ll see what happens next.” She grins toward a kaithrit man who looks like he would rather be anywhere else. “Tonn here rigged a device to jam the bots’ sensors and keep them from finding us, but it’s only got a little more charge, so I’ll make this quick.” Redd gives the camera a very serious look. “The ship’s yours now, Monika. Rebuild it, sell it, take it apart, I don’t care. But I always felt bad that we left the lot of you on Dhaal with nothing but a busted-ass ship and each other.” She glances away as the crew behind her goes silent. “We were family. I shouldn’t have done that and I’m sorry. But we can’t stay with the Mastodon, not after all this, so hopefully it’ll do you more good than it ever did for me” She looks into the camera and smirks again. “And hopefully you haven’t gone too legit on that half-baked rock,” Redd says with a grin. “It’s no fun anyway.” “Maybe another minute, Captain,” Tonn says, the kaithrit man glancing back and forth nervously. “Permission to say we should leave?” “Everybody into the pods,” Redd says, then nods to [flags.MASTODON_ASA_LOG|Asa, her ausar first mate|an ausar woman with a mechanical arm]. “Verify the coords are set before launch. I’ll be there in a sec.” A gabilani woman with an enormous chest looks up at Redd as she’s heading for one of the pods. “Tell your friends if they find my porn stash I want it back!” Redd chuckles. “Val says if you find her porn stash, it’s yours,” she says. “That’s not what I—” Val shouts as the ausar woman shoves her into an escape pod, then follows her inside. Redd looks into the camera one last time. “Time to go,” she says. “I hope this gets to you, and I hope you make something out of the ship. And I hope we see each other again someday.” The rahn woman drops a hand to her waist, and points her belt buckle at the camera. The buckle is a solid piece of shining metal with “RR” stamped on it in squarish letters. “Redd’s Ruthless forever, girls,” Redd says. She smiles into the camera, then winks. The video goes black.
context: <<POS:33144439>> textify(['\\n\\t\\tThe video blinks to life, and you see [flags'...])

original: This is the ship’s medbay, with plain white walls and a sickbed with side panels that can be raised or lowered to accommodate species of various sizes. The place is fairly well-stocked, and the storage bins are labeled with what they hold and what species it’s good for. Some of the bins hang open, their contents scattered across the floor. The room smells like antiseptic and gauze.
context: <<POS:33149638>> textify(['\\n\\tThis is the ship’s medbay, with plain white wa'...])

original: The metal-walled hallway is much like the rest of the ship, simple and utilitarian. Scrapes and scuffs mar the walls, and black marks run down either side of the hall, like someone tried to drive a tank through the place. From here, you can see a life support area to the port side, and what looks like small living quarters on the starboard side.
context: <<POS:33150151>> textify(['\\n\\tThe metal-walled hallway is much like the rest'...])

original: A short hallway holds the crew’s quarters. The quarters are built to take advantage of the ship’s height, with the rooms stacked on top of each other like apartments. Two doors on each side stand open, leading to small rooms with simple bunks and enough storage for one. A single larger room is at the end of the hallway, though the door is shut. The sign by that door reads ‘First Mate’.\n\t\n\tOne room looks bare, like whoever lived there had hardly moved in. There’s a datapad on the floor, the screen showing what looks like a basic programming interface. You can investigate it if you want.
context: <<POS:33150620>> textify(['\\n\\tA short hallway holds the crew’s quarters. The'...])

<<POS:33151281>> addButton(e++,"Datapad",(function(){clearAll(),author("Slab Bulkhead"),output(textify(_me||(_me=(0,Ce.Z)(["\n\t\tThe computer screen displays line after line of code, scrolling past in an ongoing loop. The user interface seems pretty basic, and it only takes you a second to find the button to make t...

original:
The computer screen displays line after line of code, scrolling past in an ongoing loop. The user interface seems pretty basic, and it only takes you a second to find the button to make the scrolling stop. Once that’s done, you take a look at the code.
		
		[var0
			|It doesn’t take you long to figure out what happened here. The first part is a massive attack designed to override the Pyrexxx robots’ programming, so they’ll activate and obey the scripted commands. The second part moves some of them into specific positions and directs others to patrol. It seems pretty solid so far, but after that, it gets... interesting.
			
			The code directs the robots to seek out and destroy the crew, but the initial override screws around with their sensory arrays so much that the code has to spell out how they’re supposed to hunt down their targets. And that has some serious issues. The robots require visual contact, and their other senses are practically blinded due to the override.
			
			Plus, there’s a specific number of crew in the code, and the robots are supposed to stand down once they’re all dead. But that means the robots will keep hunting until they find all the crew, which explains why they’ve been yelling that they found someone before they attack you — they think you’re part of the crew.
			
			It reads like the person who did this knew what they were doing but didn’t know any other way to make it happen aside from brute force. It’s serviceable work, but sloppy, and you get the impression whoever wrote this code was in a real hurry.
			|Most of it is endless lines of symbols and half-words that don’t make much sense to you. You scroll through it, frowning as you try to figure it out, and after a while, you can piece together the basics and make a guess about what happened here.
			
			There’s some kind of repeated command all through the early part — maybe that’s what made the Pyrexxx robots activate and start attacking everyone, as that seems like the biggest step and the kind of thing that would come first. After that, there’s three sets of code blocks that have some of the same parts but some different parts. Maybe one of them is for the regular robots, another for those turrets, and a third for something else.
			
			You scroll farther through, and see some more terms written in plain language, with ‘visual detection’ and ‘converge’ showing up a few times. Were the robots set to only detect the crew visually? That seems like a strange way to do it, but maybe whoever hacked into them didn’t have many choices.
			
			A ways further down, there’s something about the crew, and a specific number for how many crew there were. It looks like whoever wrote this wanted to make sure a certain number of people were dead. If a saboteur did this, they could make sure everyone but them got killed, if they hid until the robots were done killing the crew. So that makes sense.
			
			Beyond that, it’s hard to tell what the code is meant for, but you have some idea of how things played out. Though you haven’t seen any bodies. So maybe the crew escaped and the robots are still hunting them down, which would explain why they’re not only trying to kill you but shouting when they see you, like you’re their target.
		]
		
		[pc.isBimbo|Wow! You have, like, no idea how you managed to figure that all out on your own. It’s weird to think you’re actually smart sometimes!
		
		]After you’ve learned as much as you can from the code, you push the button to turn off the tablet. Time to get back to work.
context: <<POS:33151379>> textify(['\\n\\t\\tThe computer screen displays line after line'...])

original: A short hallway holds the crew’s quarters. The quarters are built to take advantage of the ship’s height, with the rooms stacked on top of each other like apartments. Two doors on each side stand halfway open, leading to small rooms with simple bunks and enough storage for one. A single larger room is at the end of the hallway, though the door is shut. The sign by that door reads ‘Captain’.\n\t\n\tOne room’s door hangs wide open. You can investigate it if you want.

Dirty clothes and spare machine parts litter the floor, and underwear made for a very well-endowed woman sticks out of the tiny closet.\n\t\t\n\t\tWhoever lived here clearly didn’t clean up very often. You can search the place, if you think you’ll find anything interesting.

One of them is a bra still in its packaging, advertising its incredible stretching pro...

original: You rummage through the mess, and find two things that could be useful among the domestic wreckage. One of them is a bra still in its packaging, advertising its incredible stretching properties and promising that it’ll fit “Any cup size from ‘ehh’ to ‘OMG!’”. The other is a small data drive, one your codex can easily read, with the words “VAL’S PORN” written above a cartoonish mustache.\n\t\t\t\n\t\t\tYou take both items.
context: <<POS:33156577>> textify(['\\n\\t\\t\\tYou rummage through the mess, and find two'...])

original:
The hallway continues through the center of the ship. Something heavy has been through here recently, if the long black streaks on the floor are any sign. The metal walls show a number of scrapes and scratches, and down near the floor, someone has scribbled “go suck a gryvain”.
	
	The word “OKAY!” is written next to it in a different hand.
	
	To the starboard side, you can see what looks like a recreation room of some kind. Small living quarters are in the other direction.
context: <<POS:33157421>> textify(['\\n\\tThe hallway continues through the center of th'...])

original: This part of the ship holds a small but functional rec room. A few monitors with computer consoles stand along one wall, their screens blank, broken VR goggles attached to them on curling cords. A pool table made out of spare parts is bolted to the floor, though there’s no sign of any balls or cues. Several well-used couches line the rest of the walls, some surrounding a simple metal table. A larger screen is bolted to the wall, flickering and full of static.
context: <<POS:33158035>> textify(['\\n\\tThis part of the ship holds a small but functi'...])

original: This part of the steel-walled hallway leads toward the front of the ship. Like most of the Mastodon, it shows signs of heavy use. A sticker advertising some junk shop you’ve never heard of is stuck to the starboard wall near the floor. Occasional rattles from the air system echo through the hall.\n\t\n\tThe ruined remains of the turret bots lie against the walls here, collapsed on themselves after your assault. Occasional sparks spray out of the wreckage.
context: <<POS:33158651>> textify(['\\n\\tThis part of the steel-walled hallway leads to'...])

original: Two turret bots stand in the hallway, blocking access to the rest of the Mastodon. Both of them are built like some kind of tauric tripod, with two narrow legs in the front and a wider one in the back. A hardy, tank-like body holds the legs together, and a torso better suited for some kind of military sexbot rises from that, with a sensor array on the abdomen and two surge-absorbing bio-cushions above that, leading up to a head that looks like a stylized, domed helmet. Two multi-cannons jut out where the arms should be, each with five barrels. A multi-barreled minigun points out from between the tripod’s front legs. The turrets aim their cannons at you, blaring a threat from their vocal units. “REMAINING CREW DETECTED. DESTROY ALL REMAINING CREW.” It’s a fight!
context: <<POS:33159257>> textify(['\\n\\tTwo turret bots stand in the hallway, blocking'...])

original: Sparks burst up from between the turret bot’s bio-cushions, blasting its helmeted head with hot steel. The tripod rears back, and a metallic screech erupts from its vocal unit. The robot teeters back and forth, then crashes to the ground, crushing one of its multi-cannons with its own weight. The minigun hangs limp between its front legs as the machine powers down permanently.
context: <<POS:33160245>> textify(['\\n\\t\\tSparks burst up from between the turret bot’'...])

original: You crumple to the ground, unable to keep fighting. The tripod bot that shot you down approaches, and tilts its head down at you, the lens of its helmet-like head flickering in the Mastodon’s lights. The turret takes only a moment to deploy its minigun and point it directly at your face. The barrels begin to spin. The bot booms, “REMAINING CREW DETECTED. DESTROY ALL REMAINING CREW.” The minigun’s muzzle flare is the last thing you see. GAME OVER
context: <<POS:33161238>> textify(['\\n\\t\\tYou crumple to the ground, unable to keep fi'...])

original:
The cargo bay narrows to a hallway leading toward the front of the ship. All the walls are sturdy metal, unadorned except for small dents in the walls and the scuffs that come with hauling heavy cargo. But the whole place seems intact and sturdy. Considering the beating the ship has taken, it must be even stronger than it looks to still be in one piece.
	
	[flags.MASTODON_PUZZLE
		|A reinforced door stands open at the end of the hallway, allowing access to the rest of the ship.
		|A reinforced door stands closed at the end of the hallway, cutting off access to the rest of the ship. You could try to hack it open if you want.
	] Charred marks from weapon fire mar the steel.
	
	A small screen with a keyboard beneath it blinks next to the door. There’s a message that says a video is available for viewing.
context: <<POS:33162582>> textify(['\\n\\tThe cargo bay narrows to a hallway leading tow'...])

It looks like it was shot from a camera on the screen you’re looking at — you can see the cargo bay behind you on the screen. Also on the screen is an ausar wom...

<<POS:33167665>> addButton(e++,"Screen",(function(){clearAll(),author("Slab Bulkhead"),output(textify(Pme||(Pme=(0,Ce.Z)(["\n\t\tThe video flickers to life. It looks like it was shot from a camera on the screen you’re looking at — you can see the cargo bay behind you on the screen. Also on the screen is an ausar wom...

original: The video flickers to life. It looks like it was shot from a camera on the screen you’re looking at — you can see the cargo bay behind you on the screen. Also on the screen is an ausar woman with fair skin, brown hair, and a mechanical arm that starts at her shoulder. She’s wearing an armored vest that looks hastily donned, and she looks like she’s trying very hard to stay calm, though her ears are standing straight up. “Captain?” the ausar woman says, looking into the camera. “Captain, this is Asa. Do you copy?” “I copy, Asa,” a voice from the speaker says. “We’ve lost both engines. What the hell’s going on back there?” The ausar woman’s ears tilt down a little, and relief washes over her face. “No casualties so far,” she says. “We’re holed up in the starboard half of the cargo bay. Some kind of explosion took out the engines, then the cargo boxes opened themselves up. The robots inside started walking out on their own. All of them.” Asa glances over her shoulder. Behind her, you see a half-dozen people crouched down against the wall, likely the ship’s crew. All look scared. A few of them have small guns, but they’re holding them awkwardly, and no one looks like they’re ready to fight. “Some of the robots opened fire, others just... stood there,” Asa continues. “We took out a few, then hid as best we could. I saw the largest robot head toward the bridge, but I was able to get to the cargo bay door and seal it after that. I — I don’t know how many are still out there or if they’re doing anything. They opened fire when they saw us, but once they lost visual, they stopped attacking.” She takes a deep breath. “That’s our situation. We’re stuck here with a bunch of... killer sexbots. I don’t know what else to say.” The ship’s captain lets out a string of curses. “That explains the huge fuckoff big-titty robot in the bridge,” she says. “I’m hiding up here with three others. We can get to the hidden passage in quarters, but we need to get past that thing first.” “The passages,” Asa says, glancing aside. “I don’t know if we can get to those without the robots seeing us. I pulled arms from the cabinets, but I’m the only one here who knows which end of the gun to hold.” “Figure it out,” the captain says. “I’ve got one flash grenade, but it should be enough to fuck with that giant thing’s sensors and let us get past it. Seriously, who puts boobs on a battle droid?” She lets out a rough breath. “We’ll grab our arms from quarters, then get to the passages. Meet us inside and we’ll make for the escape pods. Got that?” Asa nods, and a look of determination settles onto her face. “Got it, captain.” She pauses. “Do you have a count of who’s with you?” There’s a moment of silence from the captain, then she rattles off a list of names. “B’elac, J-Star, and Val. Everyone else with you?” “Everyone but Rowan,” Asa says, and a frown creases the sides of her mouth. “You think he’s behind this?” The captain makes a low growling sound. “Can’t say for sure, but the ship’s too small to hide in,” she says. “So if we don’t find him in quarters, he’s in the passages. And that’s not where he should be.” “I hear you, Captain. If I see him, I’ll hold onto him.” Asa glances at one of the crew behind her, then nods. “Tonn says he might have a way to jam the bots’ sensors, but it won’t last long. We’ll hold out until you get here.” “Make sure you do,” the captain says, and there’s a kind of steady reassurance in her voice. “I’m not losing any crew if I can help it. Stay together and we’ll meet you in the passage. Redd out.” “See you soon, Captain,” the ausar woman says. “Asa out.” The video goes black.
context: <<POS:33163563>> textify(['\\n\\t\\tThe video flickers to life. It looks like it'...])

original: An airlock large enough to accommodate a small ship takes up most of the port wall here. The area that surrounds the airlock has bright yellow lines painted on the floor, with text that reads “LOADING AREA - KEEP CLEAR” in several languages. This is clearly where the crew hauled large cargo in or out of the ship. The metal floor is covered with scuff marks and tire treads, showing that this area saw a great deal of use.\n\n\tSecurity cameras point down from the ceiling, and a computer console stands on one wall, probably to keep track of inventory and other transport details.
context: <<POS:33167865>> textify(['\\n\\tAn airlock large enough to accommodate a small'...])

original: This is the port section of the Mastodon’s cargo bay, and it’s less cluttered than the other areas. Clusters of bound-up netting hang from the ceiling, and boxes for smaller storage lie tumbled across the floor, but the center of this area looks like it was cleared and uncluttered. A wide door to port takes up most of the wall.
context: <<POS:33168577>> textify(['\\n\\tThis is the port section of the Mastodon’s car'...])

original: The cargo bay opens up around you, leading to the rear part of the ship. From here, you can see most of the area, and it’s filled with broken crates and battered boxes, spilling their contents across the metal floor. The Pyrexxx Industreez logo adorns several pieces of the wreckage. Lights hanging from the ceiling flicker on and off at random intervals, casting strange shadows everywhere.
context: <<POS:33169036>> textify(['\\n\\tThe cargo bay opens up around you, leading to '...])

original: This is the starboard part of the Mastodon’s cargo bay. The whole room is enormous, and clearly well-used. Most of the place shows signs of heavy wear and tear, with scrapes and dents and repair patches, and the walls and floor have tie-downs for all kinds of cargo. \n\t\n\tBoxes and crates are arranged here in a U-shape, stacked on top of each other, like someone was building a barricade or a place to hide. The edges of this improvised structure are charred with what looks like blaster shots. Not only was someone hiding here, they were being shot at.
context: <<POS:33169557>> textify(['\\n\\tThis is the starboard part of the Mastodon’s c'...])

original: This part of the cargo bay lacks the clutter that covers the rest of the place. Bright red lines on the floor outline a section of the starboard wall, with “ESCAPE POD AREA — KEEP CLEAR” written in bright red letters in several common languages. The walls are free of the marks from blaster fire that scar much of the rest of the ship.\n\t\n\tThe doors on five small bays stand closed and sealed, each marked with red lights. A glance at the readouts above the doors shows that all the escape pods have been ejected. A nearby readout shows they were all set to the same coordinates, a planet not far from where the Crash Landing crew told you to go to find this ship.
context: <<POS:33170244>> textify(['\\n\\tThis part of the cargo bay lacks the clutter t'...])

original: A single enormous metal crate takes up most of the cargo area here, plain gray with the Pyrexxx Industreez logo stamped on every side. The box looks like it was opened normally - there’s no sign of forced entry. Black tread marks lead away from the box and toward the front of the ship. A few blaster marks mar the floor around it. Whatever happened here, it didn’t keep whatever was in the box from getting away.
context: <<POS:33171041>> textify(['\\n\\tA single enormous metal crate takes up most of'...])

original: You’ve reached the center of the Mastodon’s cargo bay. More than a dozen metal boxes cover the floor here, all of them laying open. The Pyrexxx Industreez logo adorns all the boxes’ sides, and some of them look like robots walked over them, as the metal is scraped and covered with rectangular dents.\n\t\n\tThere’s a single broken robot laying in a heap among the boxes, with curved hip armor and two domes on its chest. It looks like someone put a few blaster shots through its head.
context: <<POS:33171584>> textify(['\\n\\tYou’ve reached the center of the Mastodon’s ca'...])

original: This part of the cargo bay is an utter mess. Broken boxes, shattered crates, and all kinds of wrecked metal litter the floor, making it dangerous to walk through. You catch sight of broken pieces of some kind of robot, clearly the victim of blaster fire. It doesn’t look like any people were hurt here, but with the place in such disarray, it’s hard to tell.\n\t\n\tSeveral boxes with the Pyrexxx Industreez logo on them are stacked on top of each other here, forming a makeshift barricade. Black marks from blaster shots riddle the boxes.
context: <<POS:33172199>> textify(['\\n\\tThis part of the cargo bay is an utter mess. B'...])

original: Scraps of metal and other waste litter the floor here, but they’ve all been pushed away from the port-side wall. Long lines of detritus and discarded bits of metal suggest that the mess was shoved aside in a hurry. Bright red lines on the floor outline a section around the wall, with “ESCAPE POD AREA — KEEP CLEAR” written in bright red letters in several common languages.\n\t\n\tThe doors on five small bays stand closed and sealed, each marked with red lights. A glance at the readouts above the doors shows that all the escape pods have been ejected. A nearby readout shows they were all set to the same coordinates, a planet not far from where the Crash Landing crew told you to go to find this ship.
context: <<POS:33172868>> textify(['\\n\\tScraps of metal and other waste litter the flo'...])

original: This area of the ship is a vast array of screens, keyboards, and diagnostic equipment. There’s a display of the whole ship on one screen, flashing with multiple warnings. Another screen next to it displays readouts pointing to massive damage to the engines, and another shows reports of weapons discharge all throughout the cargo bay and on the bridge.\n\t\n\tThe mess from the rest of the cargo bay doesn’t seem to have reached this area, and there aren’t any signs of battle.
context: <<POS:33173704>> textify(['\\n\\tThis area of the ship is a vast array of scree'...])

original: The rear of the cargo bay looks like it was used for the crew’s larger personal items. There are several stacks of crates and boxes tied down to the floor, all of them labeled with different names, most of them with at least one kind of lock on them. One box has “HANDS OFF, MOTHERFUCKER” stenciled on it in an eye-searing shade of pink.\n\t\n\tA small speeder is locked down to the floor, with a helmet still attached to it. Mesh bags hang from the ceiling, holding smaller boxes off of the floor.
context: <<POS:33174311>> textify(['\\n\\tThe rear of the cargo bay looks like it was us'...])

original: The ship’s maintenance bay looks like a life support room for mechanics. Cabinets and boxes full of metal parts line the walls, and the few empty spaces on the walls are filled with pinned-up ship schematics, small monitors displaying mechanical diagrams, and a calendar showing an ausar woman in a bikini. It looks like whatever took down the ship sent some things here flying, as scattered parts litter the floor.\n\t\n\tOne of the monitors seems to still be functional, and there’s a video log being displayed in a small window in the corner, though the sound is off. You can play it if you want.
context: <<POS:33174932>> textify(['\\n\\tThe ship’s maintenance bay looks like a life s'...])

<<POS:33179842>> addButton(e++,"Video Log",(function(){clearAll(),author("Slab Bulkhead"),output(textify(Vme||(Vme=(0,Ce.Z)(["\n\t\tWhen the video starts, all you see is a dark green blur, which resolves into green-skinned hands as they pull away. The camera focuses on the grinning face of a gabilani woman with brig...

original: When the video starts, all you see is a dark green blur, which resolves into green-skinned hands as they pull away. The camera focuses on the grinning face of a gabilani woman with bright orange hair and half her head shaved. She’s clad in a sleeveless top and baggy pants, and the camera is angled above her, showing off tits big enough to lose your codex between. The gabilani woman blinks at the camera a few times, then puffs her chest out and grins. “Okay! Val’s log, number one, because why the fuck not?” She lets out a grating chuckle. “This is Valchenza Ulistaara Deduun, one of the Mastodon’s two newest crew members and definitely the hotter of the two.” She gives the camera a doubtful look. “Seriously, have you seen Rowan? Poor boy’s as pretty as a pile of rusted pistons and probably even less useful in the sack. But I’m not here to talk about him.” She pauses for a moment, and frowns. “Now that I think about it, I’m not really sure what I’m here to talk about. I saw the captain making her logs, so I figured I’d give it a shot, but she’s got business reasons for that. Maybe I’m just bored out here.” She shrugs, making her boobs bounce a little. “But it’s something to do, out here in the black.” Val leans against her tool bench and starts fiddling with something that holds at least a dozen gears together. “Hauling stuff from one place to another’s not that exciting, but it beats being just another junker. Least I get to see the galaxy out here, and most of the crew’s pretty cool.”[flags.TARKUS_DESTROYED| A pained look passes over the green woman’s face. “And it’s not like I could go back to Tarkus anyway. I know the ship’s still there, but... but the world’s gone. I’m not going back if there’s no world to go to.”] She spins the gears together a few more times, then leans in closer to the camera, her voice going lower. “Anyway, I think we’re hauling some heavy shit, you know what I mean? I ran the numbers on those containers, and it looks like we’ve got some military-level drone bots in there.” Her voice drops to a whisper. “G4-Z1 means something, I swear! If those people on the net who live for this stuff are right, that’s one of Pyrexxx’s new artillery bots.” Val looks excited for a moment, then rolls her eyes as the expression fades. “But if I’m right, we didn’t even get any of the cool stuff to haul. The bot freaks all say the G4-Z1’s got some major flaws with its shield generators, and some guy went on and on about this array setup that means as long as you knock out the extra generator, the whole thing’s pretty much fucked.” She gives the camera a doubtful look. “I mean, if you trust you’re getting secrets about a Pyrite Industries wanna-be from some guy who calls himself ‘Roll Fizzlebeef.’ He’s probably just making shit up.” She leans back and drums her fingers on her tits, then grins at the camera. “But I got some new stuff for my porn stash,” she says, clearly just talking about whatever comes to mind next. “Lots of people back on Tarkus were big on the homemade amateur stuff, so I’m trying to find local porn wherever we stop. Everybody’s got to have a hobby, yeah? Funny thing is, some people don’t want their asses all over the net, so they’re still passing it around on data drives.” Val lets out a jiggling laugh. “It’s like this weird-ass underground porn ring. Valchenza Ulistaara Deduun, shipment expert and porn pirate. Hell of a life, eh?” The sound of someone talking comes from somewhere off-camera, and Val’s ears twitch toward the sound. “Oh, fuck,” she says, then reaches out and picks up the camera, giving you a close-up of her cleavage before she gets it pointed toward her face again. “That’s the mess call. I gotta go eat. But this was cool, yeah? Probably do some more of these if things don’t go completely tits-up. Later!” One of the gabilani woman’s fingers closes in toward the camera, and the video ends.
context: <<POS:33175672>> textify(['\\n\\t\\tWhen the video starts, all you see is a dark'...])

original: The port engine, a powerful piece of machinery, sits dark and silent and smoking. Occasional sparks fly from the battered machinery. All the damage is in one specific area, suggesting someone blew it up. Along with the damage, there are signs of multiple repairs, metal patches and the like, showing that the crew worked hard to keep the thing functioning.\n\t\n\tA note scribbled on one wall looks to be a list of parts, with comments like ‘need this’ or ‘will last another month’ written next to them.
context: <<POS:33179976>> textify(['\\n\\tThe port engine, a powerful piece of machinery'...])

original: The starboard engine might have once propelled the Mastodon through the depths of space, but now there’s an enormous chunk of it missing. All the damage is in one specific area, suggesting some kind of explosive sabotage. Burnt pieces of machinery litter the floor.\n\t\n\tWriting on one side of what’s left of the engine catches your eye. You see that someone scribbled a message where the engine meets the wall, clearly meant for one specific person to see: “K’Lee, if I catch you humping the engine again, you’re fired. I don’t care how much it vibrates.”
context: <<POS:33180609>> textify(['\\n\\tThe starboard engine might have once propelled'...])
